items.q: What is the current best estimate for the age of the universe?
items.why: Students may hold non-scientific views or guess. The current consensus from CMB measurements and stellar ages is approximately 13.8 billion years - the time since the Big Bang.

items.q: What does cosmological redshift tell us?
items.correct: Galaxies are receding from us due to the expansion of space.
items.misconception: Galaxies are turning red as they age.
items.random_wrong: Light gets tired and loses energy.
items.why: Students take redshift literally as colour change. It's a WAVELENGTH STRETCH - light waves get longer because space itself expands while light travels, shifting visible light toward the red end of the spectrum.

items.q: Where is our Solar System located in the Milky Way?
items.correct: About two-thirds of the way out from the centre, in one of the spiral arms.
items.misconception: Right at the centre of the galaxy.
items.why: Students assume our Sun is central (Copernican habits die hard). We're in an unremarkable position about 26,000 light years from the centre, in the Orion Spur, a minor branch of the Milky Way.
The answer examiners want is: Venus. “Mercury” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many moons does Mars have?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2. “12” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many rocky inner planets are there?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
